title: SEGMENT 16 — Partial reprogramming: rewinding aging without losing identity

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Partial reprogramming: rewinding aging without losing identity
Now I want to take you to one of the most provocative and weird frontier ideas to come out of iPSC research.
It's the idea of partial reprogramming, and it goes like this.
The OSKM factors don't just reset a cell's identity.
They also, in the process, reset a lot of the cell's epigenetic markers — including the markers we associate with aging.
Old cells have particular patterns of DNA methylation, particular chromatin marks, particular accumulated damage signatures.
When OSKM is applied long enough to fully reprogram the cell to pluripotency, all of those age markers get wiped clean.
The resulting iPSC, even if derived from a 90-year-old skin cell, looks epigenetically young.
The cellular clock has been turned back to zero.
That's interesting.
But it's also a clinical dead end, because making your skin cells pluripotent doesn't help you — pluripotent cells in your tissues form teratomas.
You don't want to be a pluripotent ball of cells.
You want to be an old skin cell that's young again.
So in 2016, a group at the Salk Institute led by Juan Carlos Izpisúa Belmonte did an experiment.
What if you applied OSKM only briefly?
What if you turned it on for a few days, then off, before the cells lost their original identity?
Could you rewind the aging clock without rewinding all the way to pluripotency?
The answer, surprisingly, was yes.
They engineered mice in which OSKM could be turned on by giving them a drug.
The mice had progeria — a genetic condition causing premature aging.
When the researchers gave the drug intermittently, in short pulses, the mice's cells showed reversal of aging markers — their skin looked younger, their organs functioned better, they lived longer.
And the cells didn't form tumors, because the OSKM was off most of the time.
Brief, cyclic application of the factors was enough to partially rewind the cells' epigenetic age without losing their identity.
The analogy is: rewind a little, not all the way — cyclic OSKM expression in old mice restores youthful gene expression without making cancer.
This work, replicated and extended over the past decade, has launched a whole subfield of partial reprogramming for rejuvenation.
The idea is that the developmental tape doesn't have to be rewound all the way — you can just back it up a few steps, refreshing the cell's epigenetic state without erasing its identity.
The cell remains a skin cell, or a liver cell, or whatever, but a younger version of itself.
Companies have formed around this concept.

Altos Labs, which launched in 2022 with several billion dollars in funding from various billionaires, has hired some of the leading reprogramming researchers — including Yamanaka himself as a senior scientific advisor — to develop partial reprogramming as a therapy.
Calico, owned by Alphabet, is working in adjacent space.
Many smaller startups are exploring different combinations of reprogramming factors, different delivery methods, different cyclic protocols.
Is this going to work as actual rejuvenation therapy for humans?
The honest answer is we don't know yet.
The mouse data is suggestive but mouse aging is not human aging.
The safety profile is critical — you absolutely cannot have a few cells go all the way to pluripotency and form tumors.
The dosing has to be precise, the duration controlled, the cell types responsive.
And the deepest question — whether epigenetic rewinding actually translates to functional rejuvenation, whether old organs can be made to work like young ones — is open.
But the conceptual seed is real and remarkable: Yamanaka's reprogramming factors don't just reset identity, they reset age.
Whether we can use that for medicine, we will find out in the next decade or two.
